learning_objectives: Compare governance frameworks: COBIT, ISO 27001, NIST CSF
Define the role of the CISO and security governance structure
Align security strategy with business objectives

lecture_notes.introduction: Security governance ensures that cybersecurity decisions are made at the right organizational level, aligned with business strategy, and supported with appropriate resources.
key_concepts: NIST CSF 2.0: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover
ISO 27001: ISMS establishment, implementation, maintenance
COBIT: governance and management objectives for enterprise IT
CISO reporting structure and organizational placement
Security steering committee and board-level reporting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping an organization's security program to NIST CSF 2.0, identifying maturity levels for each function

review.value: Added 'Govern' function: establishes organizational context, risk management strategy, roles/responsibilities, and policy. Elevates governance as the foundation for all other security functions.
International standard for Information Security Management Systems. Requires systematic risk assessment, control implementation, and continuous improvement. Certification demonstrates due diligence.

learning_objectives: Draft organizational security policies following industry standards
Distinguish between policies, standards, guidelines, and procedures
Implement a policy lifecycle with review and enforcement
readings: NIST SP 800-12 Introduction to Computer Security

lecture_notes.introduction: Policies are the foundation of a security program. They define what the organization requires, standards define how to meet those requirements, and procedures define the step-by-step implementation.
key_concepts: Policy hierarchy: policies, standards, guidelines, procedures
Essential policies: acceptable use, access control, incident response, data classification
Policy development lifecycle: draft, review, approve, distribute, enforce, update
Policy enforcement mechanisms and exception processes
Metrics for policy compliance measurement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drafting an organizational data classification policy with supporting standards for each classification level

review.value: Policy: 'All sensitive data must be encrypted at rest.' Standard: 'AES-256-GCM with keys managed in the approved KMS.' The policy states what; the standard states how.
Business needs sometimes conflict with policy. A formal exception process documents the risk, requires management approval, defines compensating controls, and sets a review date.

learning_objectives: Design a compliance program covering multiple regulatory frameworks
Manage audit processes and evidence collection
readings: SOC 2 Type II audit preparation guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Organizations face overlapping compliance requirements from multiple regulations. An effective compliance program maps controls to requirements efficiently, avoiding duplicate effort.
key_concepts: Control mapping: one control satisfying multiple regulations
GRC platforms for compliance management
Continuous compliance monitoring vs point-in-time audits
Evidence collection and audit trail maintenance
Audit preparation: readiness assessments, evidence gathering
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a unified control matrix that maps NIST CSF, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS, and SOC 2 to a single set of organizational controls

review.value: A single control implementation can satisfy multiple regulations. Example: 'Encrypt sensitive data at rest with AES-256' satisfies PCI-DSS Req 3.4, HIPAA technical safeguard, ISO 27001 A.8.24, and NIST CSF PR.DS-1.
Automated scanning and monitoring that continuously validates control effectiveness, replacing annual point-in-time assessments with real-time compliance dashboards.

learning_objectives: Design an effective security awareness training program
Measure security culture through behavioral metrics
Implement phishing simulation and social engineering training
readings: NIST SP 800-50 Building an Awareness Program

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology cannot compensate for untrained users. A strong security culture where employees are an active defense layer is more effective than any single technical control.
key_concepts: Security awareness program design: audience analysis, content, delivery, measurement
Phishing simulation programs: baseline, training, progressive difficulty
Role-based training: developers, executives, general staff
Behavioral change metrics vs completion metrics
Gamification and positive reinforcement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a phishing simulation campaign with progressive difficulty, analyzing results, and creating targeted follow-up training

review.value: Phishing click rate alone is insufficient. Measure: report rate (do employees report suspicious emails?), time-to-report, repeat offender rate, and self-reported near-miss incidents.
Executives face targeted spear-phishing (CEO fraud, BEC). Training must include real-world examples of executive-targeted attacks and cover wire transfer verification procedures.

learning_objectives: Develop meaningful security metrics for different audiences
Build executive dashboards that drive decision-making
Communicate cyber risk in business terms
readings: How to Measure Anything in Cybersecurity Risk

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective security metrics bridge the gap between technical security operations and business decision-making. The right metrics at the right level drive investment and accountability.
key_concepts: Operational metrics: patch compliance, vulnerability age, MTTD/MTTR
Strategic metrics: risk reduction, security investment ROI, maturity score
Board-level reporting: risk exposure in financial terms, trend analysis
Metric selection: actionable, measurable, relevant, timely
Benchmarking against industry peers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a board-level cybersecurity dashboard from operational data, translating technical metrics into business risk language

review.value: Boards understand risk, compliance, and financial impact. Translate: 'We have 200 unpatched critical vulnerabilities' into 'We have a $2.4M annualized risk exposure from unpatched systems, reducible to $200K with a $150K patching initiative.'
CMM-based maturity (Initial, Repeatable, Defined, Managed, Optimizing) provides a roadmap for security program improvement and benchmarking against peer organizations.

class_schedule.topic: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Governance
learning_objectives: Develop a business continuity plan aligned with organizational risk tolerance
Design disaster recovery strategies for critical systems
Conduct BCP testing and exercise programs

lecture_notes.introduction: Business continuity and disaster recovery ensure organizational survival when security controls fail. Governance ensures these plans are maintained, tested, and funded.
key_concepts: Business Impact Analysis (BIA): identifying critical processes and dependencies
RPO/RTO determination per business function
DR strategies: hot, warm, cold sites, cloud-based DR
BCP testing: tabletop, walkthrough, simulation, full interruption
Plan maintenance: annual reviews, change-triggered updates
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a Business Impact Analysis and developing DR strategies with RPO/RTO targets for each critical business function

review.value: Identify critical business processes, determine maximum tolerable downtime (MTPD), identify dependencies (systems, people, vendors), quantify financial impact of downtime. Drives RPO/RTO targets.
Tabletop exercises quarterly, functional walkthrough semi-annually, simulation exercise annually, full interruption test every 2-3 years. Each level tests different aspects of the plan.

questions.question: A company has achieved ISO 27001 certification but still suffered a major data breach due to an unpatched vulnerability that existed for 8 months. Explain how this is possible, and what governance failures it reveals despite certification.
questions.answer: ISO 27001 certification validates that an ISMS is established and controls are defined, but does not guarantee that every control operates effectively at all times. The breach reveals several governance failures: (1) The vulnerability management process (ISO 27001 A.8.8) was defined but not effectively implemented or monitored. (2) Continuous monitoring (clause 9.1) was inadequate: the 8-month gap indicates no one tracked patch compliance against SLAs. (3) Management review (clause 9.3) did not surface the patching gap as a risk indicator. (4) Internal audit (clause 9.2) either missed the gap or findings were not remediated. Certification is a point-in-time assessment; governance must ensure continuous compliance between audits.

rubric.key: Identifies specific governance failures with ISO clauses

questions.question: Draft a two-sentence information security policy statement for 'Encryption of Data at Rest' and then write the corresponding standard that would implement this policy. Include specific algorithms, key lengths, and key management requirements.
questions.answer: Policy: 'All data classified as Confidential or higher must be encrypted at rest using approved encryption methods. Encryption keys must be managed through the organization's approved key management system.' Standard: 'Encryption algorithm: AES-256-GCM (NIST approved). Key generation: FIPS 140-2 validated CSPRNG. Key storage: Hardware Security Module (HSM) or approved cloud KMS (AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault) with FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validation. Key rotation: annually for data-encrypting keys, every 3 years for key-encrypting keys. Key access: restricted to the encryption service account, with dual-person integrity for HSM administrative access. Implementation: full-disk encryption (BitLocker/LUKS) for endpoints, TDE for databases, S3/blob encryption with CMK for cloud storage.'

rubric.key: Clear policy statement separating what from how
Standard with specific algorithms and key lengths

questions.question: A CISO must present the security program's status to the board of directors. The operational data shows: 2,400 critical vulnerabilities, MTTD of 12 days, phishing click rate of 18%, and 3 incidents in the past quarter. Translate these into a board-level briefing with business risk context and investment recommendations.
questions.answer: Board briefing: 'Our current cyber risk exposure is elevated in three areas: (1) Vulnerability exposure: 2,400 critical vulnerabilities represent an estimated $8.5M annualized risk (based on FAIR analysis of breach probability). A $500K investment in automated patching would reduce this to $1.2M within 90 days. (2) Detection capability: Our 12-day detection gap means attackers have nearly two weeks of undetected access. Industry median is 7 days. Investing $300K in EDR and SIEM tuning would reduce MTTD to 4 days. (3) Human risk: 18% phishing click rate is 3x industry average, indicating our workforce is our largest attack surface. A $150K enhanced awareness program with quarterly simulations targets 5% within 12 months. Recommendation: $950K security investment with projected 70% risk reduction and ROI achievable within fiscal year.'

rubric.key: Translates operational metrics to business risk
Includes financial context and investment justification

questions.question: A business impact analysis reveals that the e-commerce platform has an MTPD of 4 hours and generates $50K/hour in revenue. The current DR plan has an RTO of 24 hours using cold-site recovery. Calculate the gap and recommend a DR strategy upgrade with cost-benefit analysis.
questions.answer: Gap analysis: MTPD = 4 hours, current RTO = 24 hours. Gap = 20 hours. Potential loss if DR is invoked: $50K x 24 hours = $1.2M (current). Acceptable loss: $50K x 4 hours = $200K. Excess risk: $1M per incident. Recommended upgrade: Hot standby with database replication (RTO: 15 minutes, RPO: near-zero). Cost estimate: $200K/year for cloud-based hot standby infrastructure. Cost-benefit: If a DR event occurs once every 2 years (ARO=0.5), current ALE = $1.2M x 0.5 = $600K. With hot standby ALE = $12.5K x 0.5 = $6.25K. Annual risk reduction: $593.75K. Net savings: $593.75K - $200K = $393.75K/year. ROI: 197%. The investment is strongly justified.

major_assignments.title: Security Governance Program Design
major_assignments.description: Design a complete security governance program for a provided organizational scenario including framework selection, policy development, compliance mapping, metrics, and board reporting.
objectives: Select and customize a governance framework
Develop core security policies and standards
Design a compliance program with unified controls
deliverables: Core security policy set (5 policies with supporting standards)
Executive dashboard with sample quarterly report

criteria: Five core policies covering key domains
Meaningful metrics for operational and strategic audiences
Comprehensive governance program aligned with business objectives
Professional policy set following industry standards
Executive reporting that drives security investment decisions

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of governance, compliance, and policy concepts

lab_assignments.description: Policy drafting, compliance mapping, and metrics exercises

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering frameworks, policies, and compliance

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all governance and compliance topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Group discussions encouraged. All policy documents and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Policies must be original work. Using templates as a starting point is acceptable but the content must be customized and substantive.
course_policies.professional_conduct: Governance discussions should reflect professional standards. Respect organizational confidentiality in case study discussions.
